step.title: Step 17
step.actionDisplay: UP -> RIGHT -> DOWN -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 8 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, RIGHT, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 83, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: UP worked - agent moved from 30-35 to 24-29. Blocks stayed in place. Key insight: blocks only move when agent is holding another block (path extended). When holding a block, moving the agent shifts unheld blocks but held block stays. Current: Agent 24-29, Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 25-28. Target: Red, Green, Blue.
- Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
- Plan: Grab Blue (middle block), move agent to reposition, then retract to place Blue at new position. This should swap Blue and Green.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 82 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze what happened:
After UP: Agent at 18-23, blocks unchanged
After RIGHT (grab Blue): Blue moved from cols 18-21 to 24-27 (grabbed!)
After DOWN: Agent at 24-29, Red stayed at 13-16, Blue stayed at 19-22 (cols 24-27), Green moved from 25-28 to 31-34
After retract (LEFT x5): Blue stayed at 19-22, cols 24-27 (didn't return to cols 18-21)
So the retract didn't place Blue at the agent's position. Blue is still at cols 24-27. This means the retract didn't work as expected.
Wait, looking more carefully:
- After DOWN: Blue at 19-22, cols 24-27 (still grabbed)
- After retract: Blue at 19-22, cols 24-27 (still grabbed?)
The retract didn't release Blue. Maybe I need to retract more LEFT actions, or maybe the retract works differently.
Actually, looking at the output, after retract, Blue is still at cols 24-27. This means the path is still extended to Blue. The retract didn't work.
Let me check: I did LEFT x5. Maybe I need more LEFT actions to fully retract. Or maybe the retract only works when the agent is aligned with the block.
Current state:
- Agent: 24-29
- Red: 13-16, cols 18-21
- Blue: 19-22, cols 24-27 (still grabbed)
- Green: 31-34, cols 18-21
The order is now Red, Blue, Green (top to bottom). But I wanted Red, Green, Blue. Green moved down to 31-34, which is below Blue at 19-22. So the order is still Red, Blue, Green.
Wait, but Green moved from 25-28 to 31-34 (down 6 rows). Blue stayed at 19-22. So the order is:
- Red: 13-16
- Blue: 19-22
- Green: 31-34
This is still Red, Blue, Green. I wanted Red, Green, Blue.
The issue is that when I moved DOWN, Green moved down but Blue stayed. So Green went from being below Blue to being even further below Blue. This didn't help swap them.
Let me think about this differently. To swap Blue and Green:
- Current: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 31-34
- Target: Red 13-16, Green ?, Blue ?
I need Green to be between Red and Blue. Currently Green is at 31-34, which is below Blue at 19-22.
One approach: Grab Green, move agent UP past Blue, then retract. This would place Green above Blue.
But wait, Blue is still grabbed (cols 24-27). I need to release Blue first. Let me try retracting more LEFT actions.
Actually, looking at the earlier successful retract:
- After extending RIGHT: Green at cols 42-45
- After retracting LEFT x10: Green at cols 18-21 (released)
So I need to retract enough LEFT actions to fully release the block. Let me try more LEFT actions.
But first, let me check the current state more carefully. Blue is at cols 24-27, which is only 6 cols to the right of 18-21. So maybe I only need a few LEFT actions to release it.
Let me try retracting more LEFT actions to release Blue, then try a different strategy.
Actually, I realize the issue. When I moved DOWN, Green moved down but Blue stayed. The order became Red, Blue, Green (with Green further down). To get Red, Green, Blue, I need to:
1. Release Blue (retract LEFT)
2. Grab Green
3. Move agent UP past Blue
4. Retract to place Green above Blue
But wait, after step 3, the agent would be above Blue, and Green would stay at its position. Then retracting would place Green at the agent's position (above Blue). But Red would also move up.
Let me trace:
Current: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22 (cols 24-27, grabbed), Green 31-34
Step 1: Retract LEFT to release Blue
- Blue returns to cols 18-21
Step 2: Move DOWN to align with Green (31-34): Agent at 30-35
Step 3: Grab Green (extend RIGHT)
Step 4: Move UP twice - Red and Blue move up, Green stays
- Agent: 18-23
- Red: 1-10 (moved up 12)
- Blue: 7-16 (moved up 12)
- Green: 31-34 (stays)
Step 5: Retract LEFT - Green placed at agent's position (18-23)
Result:
- Red: 1-10
- Blue: 7-16
- Green: 18-23
Order: Red, Blue, Green (still wrong!)
Hmm, the relative order of Red and Blue is maintained, and Green is placed below them. I can't change the relative order of the unheld blocks.
Wait, I think I'm misunderstanding the mechanic. Let me reconsider.
When I grab a block and move the agent, the unheld blocks move with the agent. The held block stays. When I retract, the held block is placed at the agent's position.
So if I grab Green and move UP, Red and Blue move up but Green stays. Then when I retract, Green is placed at the agent's position.
But the issue is that Red and Blue maintain their relative order. So if Red is above Blue before the move, Red is still above Blue after the move. And Green is placed at the agent's position, which could be anywhere.
To get Red, Green, Blue:
- Red should be at the top
- Green should be in the middle
- Blue should be at the bottom
Current: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 31-34
If I grab Blue and move DOWN, Red and Green move down but Blue stays. Then when I retract, Blue is placed at the agent's position.
Let me trace:
Current: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22 (grabbed), Green 31-34
Step 1: Move DOWN - Red and Green move down, Blue stays
- Agent: 30-35
- Red: 19-22 (moved down 6)
- Green: 37-40 (moved down 6)
- Blue: 19-22 (stays)
But Red and Blue would both be at 19-22. Collision!
Hmm, this is problematic. Let me try a different approach.
Actually, wait. Looking at the output after DOWN:
- Red: 13-16 (didn't move!)
- Blue: 19-22 (stays, grabbed)
- Green: 31-34 (moved down 6)
So Red didn't move! Only Green moved. This is strange. Earlier, when I moved UP, both Red and Blue moved. But now, when I moved DOWN, only Green moved.
Oh, I see. Red is at 13-16, which is near the top of the playable area. Maybe Red can't move further up, so it stayed. But when I moved DOWN, Red should have moved down, not stayed.
Wait, let me re-read the output:
- After DOWN: Red at 13-16, Blue at 19-22, Green at 31-34
Before DOWN:
- Red at 13-16, Blue at 19-22, Green at 25-28
So Green moved from 25-28 to 31-34 (down 6). Red and Blue stayed.
But earlier, when I moved UP (with Green grabbed):
- Before UP: Red 19-22, Blue 25-28, Green 31-34 (grabbed)
- After UP: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 31-34 (stays, grabbed)
So Red moved from 19-22 to 13-16 (up 6), Blue moved from 25-28 to 19-22 (up 6), Green stayed.
So the rule is: when the agent moves, unheld blocks move in the same direction. But there might be constraints (e.g., blocks can't go outside the playable area).
In the current case, when I moved DOWN with Blue grabbed:
- Red stayed at 13-16 (didn't move down)
- Green moved from 25-28 to 31-34 (moved down 6)
Why didn't Red move? Maybe because Red is "blocked" by something, or maybe Red is also considered "held" for some reason.
Actually, wait. Let me check if Red is connected to the agent via the path. Looking at the adjacency list from earlier:
- 1(c) -- 2(R) - dark gray connected to Red
- 1(c) -- 5(b) - dark gray connected to Blue
- 1(c) -- 8(N) - dark gray connected to Green
- 1(c) -- 11(M) - dark gray connected to agent
All blocks are connected to the dark gray area, and the agent is connected to the dark gray area. So all blocks should move when the agent moves (unless held).
But in the current case, Red didn't move when I moved DOWN. This is strange.
Oh wait, maybe the issue is that Red is at the top of the playable area and can't move further up. But I moved DOWN, so Red should have moved down, not up.
Let me re-examine. When I moved DOWN with Blue grabbed:
- Red stayed at 13-16
- Blue stayed at 19-22 (grabbed)
- Green moved from 25-28 to 31-34
So only Green moved. This suggests that Red is somehow "blocked" or "anchored".
Actually, I think I see the issue. Red might be considered "held" because the path connects to it. Let me check if the path is still connected to Red.
Looking at the current state, the agent is at 24-29, and Blue is grabbed (cols 24-27). The path extends from the agent to Blue. But what about Red? Is the path still connected to Red?
Actually, I think the path is a single line that extends from the agent to the right. When I extend RIGHT, the path connects to the block at the agent's row. When I retract LEFT, the path retracts.
So the path only connects to one block at a time. When I grabbed Blue, the path disconnected from any other block.
But then why didn't Red move when I moved DOWN? Red should have moved because it's unheld.
Unless... Red is "anchored" because it's at the top of the playable area. Or maybe there's a different rule.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to understand all the rules, let me just try to achieve the goal: Red, Green, Blue order.
Current: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22 (grabbed), Green 31-34
First, I need to release Blue. Let me try retracting more LEFT actions.
Then, I'll try to grab Green and move the agent to place Green between Red and Blue.
Actually, let me first release Blue and then assess the situation.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: After grabbing Blue and moving DOWN, only Green moved (25-28 → 31-34), Red stayed at 13-16. Blue still grabbed at cols 24-27. Retract didn't release Blue. Need to release Blue first, then try different strategy.
Plan: Release Blue by retracting more LEFT, then grab Green and reposition.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22 (cols 24-27, grabbed), Green 31-34, Agent 24-29
# Need to release Blue first

# Try retracting more LEFT
predict(expect_change=True, note='Retract more to release Blue')
action(['LEFT'] * 5)

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== After more retract ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Check if Blue was released (should be at cols 18-21)
# If not, try even more LEFT
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
=== After more retract ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 24-29
